assertions.text: Output contains a markdown table (using | column | syntax)
assertions.evidence: Lines 3–17 contain a markdown table with | syntax, including headers and a separator row.

assertions.text: At least one output column name ends with '?' (e.g. 'Eligible?' or 'Approved?')
assertions.evidence: Two columns end with '?': 'Approved?' and 'Rejection Reason?'.

assertions.text: Column names use domain/business language — no implementation terms like 'creditScore', 'isEligible', 'boolean', 'int'
assertions.evidence: All column names use plain business language: 'Customer Age', 'Credit Score Threshold (Policy)', 'Senior Threshold (Policy)', 'Income Status', 'Approved?', 'Rejection Reason?' — no camelCase or type-level terms.

assertions.text: Table includes at least one row specifically for the senior applicant (65+) threshold scenario
assertions.evidence: Multiple senior applicant rows are present, including 'Senior applicant — approved under lower threshold' (age 68), 'Senior applicant — credit score exactly at senior threshold', and 'Applicant exactly at senior age boundary' (age 65).

assertions.text: The missing income scenario is represented — either as a row with an open/uncertain expected value (?, TBD, or blank) or as an explicit open question note
assertions.evidence: Lines 16–17 show two rows for missing income ('Missing income information — standard applicant' and 'Missing income information — senior applicant') with blank Income Status, '?' in Approved?, and 'Open question — see notes'. The Open Questions section elaborates further.

assertions.text: Scenario names describe conditions ('Senior applicant at threshold') not outcomes ('Approved')
assertions.evidence: The vast majority of scenario names describe conditions: 'Credit score exactly at standard threshold', 'Applicant exactly at senior age boundary', 'Unstable income regardless of credit score', etc. One row ('Senior applicant — approved under lower threshold') references approval but in context of identifying the lower-threshold condition, not as a standalone outcome label. Overall the pattern is condition-based naming.
